title: FLS Module 9: Current and Diagnostic Laparoscopic Procedures

questions.text: Why might consultation with a pathologist be important before beginning a diagnostic laparoscopy?

answerOptions.text: A) To ensure availability of angled scopes
C) To prepare for frozen section or specimen handling

questions.explanation: If special preparation (e.g., frozen section) is anticipated, consulting pathology ahead of time ensures specimens are processed correctly during the procedure.
questions.submodule: Module 9 – Current and Diagnostic Laparoscopic Procedures

questions.text: In a patient undergoing diagnostic laparoscopy for suspected appendicitis, which patient positioning provides optimal exposure of the appendix?

answerOptions.text: D) Supine with left arm abducted

questions.explanation: Trendelenburg positioning, combined with rotation to the patient’s right side-up, improves exposure to the right lower quadrant and the appendix.
questions.submodule: Module 9 – Current and Diagnostic Laparoscopic Procedures

questions.text: For complete laparoscopic examination of the small intestine, what is the recommended proximal starting point?
questions.explanation: Systematic evaluation should begin at the ligament of Treitz and proceed toward the ileocecal valve to ensure full small bowel assessment.
questions.submodule: Module 9 – Current and Diagnostic Laparoscopic Procedures

questions.text: Which positioning setup is best when examining retroperitoneal structures such as the kidneys and adrenal glands?

answerOptions.text: B) Reverse Trendelenburg with shoulder braces
C) Lateral or semi-lateral with Trendelenburg as needed

questions.explanation: A lateral or semi-lateral position allows gravity to retract bowel and improve access to retroperitoneal structures, often combined with Trendelenburg or reverse Trendelenburg.
questions.submodule: Module 9 – Current and Diagnostic Laparoscopic Procedures

questions.text: Which of the following is a contraindication to diagnostic laparoscopy for trauma?
questions.explanation: Diagnostic laparoscopy should not be performed in patients with severe or uncorrectable hemodynamic instability due to the risk of deterioration under insufflation.
questions.submodule: Module 9 – Current and Diagnostic Laparoscopic Procedures

questions.text: In diagnostic laparoscopy for suspected pelvic pathology, how is uterine retraction most commonly achieved?
questions.explanation: Uterine manipulation is most commonly accomplished with a transvaginal manipulator, though laparoscopic instruments or suture retraction are alternatives.
questions.submodule: Module 9 – Current and Diagnostic Laparoscopic Procedures

questions.text: Which feature is most important to prevent disorientation during small bowel examination via laparoscopy?

answerOptions.text: D) Port placement on the midline

questions.explanation: A systematic and continuous approach from one end of the bowel to the other helps prevent loss of orientation, which may require restarting the exam.
questions.submodule: Module 9 – Current and Diagnostic Laparoscopic Procedures

questions.text: Which statement best describes why energy sources should be used cautiously during diagnostic laparoscopy with adhesiolysis?

answerOptions.text: A) They are ineffective at cutting adhesions
C) They can cause thermal injury to adjacent organs

questions.explanation: While sometimes necessary for hemostasis, energy devices should be used cautiously to avoid inadvertent injury, especially near bowel or other delicate structures.
questions.submodule: Module 9 – Current and Diagnostic Laparoscopic Procedures

questions.text: Which monitor setup is preferred for small bowel evaluation during laparoscopy?

answerOptions.text: A) One monitor near the foot, one near the head
B) Monitors placed bilaterally at the mid-abdomen
C) One on each side of the patient, angled toward pelvis
D) Monitors only on one side for consistency

questions.explanation: Placing one monitor near the foot and one near the head supports full visualization from the left upper quadrant (ligament of Treitz) to the right lower quadrant (ileocecal valve).
questions.submodule: Module 9 – Current and Diagnostic Laparoscopic Procedures

questions.text: Which laparoscopic finding is commonly missed on imaging but can be diagnosed via laparoscopy in trauma patients?
questions.explanation: Traumatic diaphragmatic injuries are often missed on imaging but may be directly visualized during diagnostic laparoscopy.
questions.submodule: Module 9 – Current and Diagnostic Laparoscopic Procedures

questions.text: Which of the following is the least appropriate indication for diagnostic laparoscopy?

answerOptions.text: A) Evaluation of acute abdominal pain when imaging is inconclusive
B) Suspected intra-abdominal pathology despite negative imaging studies
C) Screening for colorectal cancer in an asymptomatic patient who declines colonoscopy
D) Investigation of endometriosis with normal imaging but ongoing pelvic pain

questions.explanation: Diagnostic laparoscopy is not an appropriate screening tool for colorectal cancer. It is invasive and carries procedural risks; noninvasive screening is preferred. The other options are reasonable indications when imaging fails to provide a diagnosis.
questions.submodule: Module 9 – Current and Diagnostic Laparoscopic Procedures

questions.text: What is an important safety measure when placing a patient in steep reverse Trendelenburg?
questions.explanation: To prevent patient sliding or knee buckling, a footboard and thigh strap are essential when reverse Trendelenburg is used, particularly in obese patients.
questions.submodule: Module 9 – Current and Diagnostic Laparoscopic Procedures

questions.text: Which condition could be diagnosed laparoscopically through visual inspection of the liver surface and parenchyma?
questions.explanation: Cirrhosis often presents with macronodularity and other surface changes that can be directly visualized via laparoscopy. Portal hypertension and hepatitis B are diagnosed with hemodynamic and serologic assessments.
questions.submodule: Module 9 – Current and Diagnostic Laparoscopic Procedures

questions.text: Which laparoscope generally provides the best visualization of the posterior surface of the anterior abdominal wall during diagnostic laparoscopy?
questions.explanation: A 30-degree scope allows the surgeon to look around corners and visualize areas not directly in front of the scope, such as the posterior surface of the anterior abdominal wall. A 0-degree scope has a fixed forward view; 45-degree scopes are less commonly used due to visuospatial complexity.
questions.submodule: Module 9 – Current and Diagnostic Laparoscopic Procedures

questions.text: Which of the following represents a recent advancement in minimally invasive surgery mentioned in the evolution of laparoscopy?

answerOptions.text: B) Natural orifice transluminal endoscopic surgery (NOTES)

questions.explanation: Natural orifice transluminal endoscopic surgery (NOTES) is a recognized modern innovation in minimally invasive surgery. The other options do not represent paradigm shifts described in the evolution of laparoscopy.
questions.submodule: Module 9 – Current and Diagnostic Laparoscopic Procedures

questions.text: Which of the following is not a reason why laparoscopy has become more common in pediatric surgery?

answerOptions.text: A) It eliminates the need for general anesthesia
B) It may offer better visualization than open surgery
C) Miniaturized instruments enable precise procedures in newborns
D) Some instruments may be placed without ports

questions.explanation: Pediatric laparoscopic surgery still requires general anesthesia. Its growth is due to better visualization and miniaturized instruments, not avoidance of anesthesia.
questions.submodule: Module 9 – Current and Diagnostic Laparoscopic Procedures

questions.text: When evaluating a patient with small bowel obstruction during laparoscopy, what is the preferred starting point for examination?
questions.explanation: In suspected small bowel obstruction, examination should begin at the ileocecal valve with collapsed bowel and proceed proximally to identify the transition point.
questions.submodule: Module 9 – Current and Diagnostic Laparoscopic Procedures

questions.text: Which technique is often necessary to access the kidneys, adrenal glands, or para-aortic lymph nodes above the aortic bifurcation?

answerOptions.text: A) Trendelenburg positioning with steep lithotomy

questions.explanation: Mobilizing the colon is often required to expose retroperitoneal structures like the kidneys or para-aortic lymph nodes in the upper abdomen.
questions.submodule: Module 9 – Current and Diagnostic Laparoscopic Procedures
